_doc: Fully synthetic, privacy-safe (no real party/firm names, contract text, or values). Each case is a brainstorm transcript closed on an issue; the rubric checks whether the MEDIUM-tier close-distillation captures the question explored, the position landed, and the fallbacks weighed (or honestly distils to None on a dismissed brainstorm). Generic terms only.

nodes.heading: Limitation of Liability
nodes.body: Each party's aggregate liability under this Agreement is limited as set out in this clause. The parties shall agree the cap and any carve-outs during negotiation.

nodes.heading: Licence Grant
nodes.body: The Provider grants the Customer a licence to use the Platform on the terms set out below. The scope, exclusivity and field of use are to be agreed.

nodes.heading: Governing Law and Jurisdiction
nodes.body: This Agreement is governed by the law agreed by the parties, and the parties submit to the agreed forum for disputes.

issue.title: Liability cap level and carve-outs
issue.our_position: Prefer a cap tied to fees paid, with carve-outs for confidentiality and IP misuse.
issue.their_position: Pushing for a higher multiple of fees and a shorter carve-out list.
issue.options_on_table: Cap at fees paid in the trailing year; a super-cap multiple; or uncapped.

transcript.question: Where should we try to land on the liability cap for this one?
transcript.answer: There are roughly three shapes worth weighing: an uncapped position, a multiple-of-fees super-cap, and a cap tied to the fees paid in the trailing year. Which risk matters most to you here?

transcript.question: Uncapped is a non-starter for us. I worry the super-cap multiple looks greedy if we open with it.
transcript.answer: Agreed. Uncapped is hard for us to live with given the exposure, and opening on a super-cap multiple reads as aggressive and tends to invite an equally aggressive counter. A cap pegged to the trailing-year fees is the more defensible anchor on the reasonableness spectrum.

transcript.question: Okay. And we'd still want confidentiality and IP misuse outside the cap.
transcript.answer: Right. The fair landing is a cap at the fees paid in the trailing year, with carve-outs for confidentiality breaches and IP misuse sitting outside the cap. That keeps the headline number reasonable while protecting the two exposures we can't afford to cap.

issue.title: Whether to grant exclusivity on the licence
issue.our_position: Reluctant to grant exclusivity; open to a narrower commitment.
issue.their_position: Asking for an exclusive licence in their territory.
issue.options_on_table: Full exclusivity; a field-of-use limited exclusivity; or non-exclusive with a most-favoured-customer assurance.

transcript.question: They want exclusivity on the licence. Should we give it?
transcript.answer: Worth pressure-testing before conceding. Full exclusivity locks us out of every other counterparty in their territory for the term, which is a heavy structural commitment. What's driving their ask?

transcript.question: They say they need it to justify their investment. But exclusivity would block us from three other prospects we're already talking to.
transcript.answer: Then full exclusivity is the option to pass over: the opportunity cost of blocking those prospects outweighs the deal. A field-of-use limited exclusivity was also on the table, but it still constrains us more than the upside warrants here.

transcript.question: So what do we offer instead?
transcript.answer: Land on a non-exclusive licence paired with a most-favoured-customer assurance, so they get comfort they won't be undercut without us giving up the territory. Exclusivity is declined; the MFC assurance is what we offer in its place.

issue.title: Which governing law and forum to push for
issue.our_position: Prefer our home jurisdiction's law and courts.
issue.their_position: Would prefer their own home forum.
issue.options_on_table: Our home jurisdiction; their home jurisdiction; or a neutral third-country seat with arbitration.

transcript.question: What should we push for on governing law and forum?
transcript.answer: Three realistic shapes: our home jurisdiction, theirs, or a neutral third-country seat under arbitration. Which matters more to you, familiarity or perceived neutrality?

transcript.question: A neutral seat sounds fair but I'm wary of the cost and the unfamiliar procedure.
transcript.answer: That's the trade. A neutral third-country arbitration seat reads as even-handed, but it adds cost and procedural unfamiliarity that tend to fall on us disproportionately as the smaller party. It's a reasonable fallback, not the opening position.

transcript.question: Then let's open on our home jurisdiction.
transcript.answer: Agreed: open on our home jurisdiction's law and courts as the position, holding the neutral third-country arbitration seat in reserve as the fallback if they refuse to concede the forum.

transcript.question: Donna, are you there?
transcript.answer: Yes, I'm here. What would you like to think through on the renewal term?

transcript.question: Actually never mind, wrong issue. I'll come back to this later.
transcript.answer: No problem. Nothing captured here; reopen this whenever you're ready to work through it.
